site.title: Vensmoen reception centre – information for residents
site.description: Permanent information for residents of Vensmoen reception centre in Saltdal: about the centre, house rules, waste sorting and a practical guide to life in Norway.
site.name: Vensmoen mottak
site.intro: Here you will find information about the centre, the house rules, waste sorting and the essentials of life in Norway. Choose your language at the top of the page.
site.comingSoon: More information will be added here.
site.dutyText: When the office is closed, call the duty phone. In urgent situations all relevant staff step in until the matter is resolved.
site.emergencyNote: Free from any phone, day and night.
site.mentalText: Going through a hard time, feeling low, or just need someone to talk to? Mental Helse answers around the clock — free and anonymous.

ui.tagline: How to sort your waste at home
ui.intro: Sort your waste into the right bin or bag, and keep the types apart. Then as much as possible can be reused.
ui.legalNote: This content is based on the national standard sortere.no.
ui.scanHint: Scan for the online version in more languages
ui.quizIntro: Where should the item go? Tap the right bin.
ui.quizBelongPrompt: Does this go in this bin?

about.title: About the centre
about.lead: Vensmoen reception centre is located at Røkland in Saltdal municipality, Nordland.
about.contactsIntro: Every matter has its own contact person. Write directly to the right one, and you will get an answer faster.
about.transportIntro: Buses and trains through Røkland run infrequently, and even less often on public holidays. Check the timetable before every trip:

blocks.heading: The office
blocks.body:
The office is open on weekdays from 08:00 to 16:00; on weekends and public holidays the building is closed.
Before coming by, email the logistics coordinator and briefly state the reason. That way the person you need will be there when you arrive – or will help you without a visit.

rules.title: House rules
rules.lead: The rules apply to everyone living at the centre and are available here in several languages.

blocks.heading: Peace, order and safety
blocks.body:
• The reception centre must be quiet from 23:00 to 06:00.
• You must not disturb others or behave in an unpleasant manner. Violence, vandalism, or other criminal acts will be reported to the police.
• Drinking alcohol in common areas is not allowed.
• Smoking, water pipes, and e-cigarettes are prohibited in common areas and in your room.
• Pets are not allowed at the reception centre.
• You must familiarize yourself with the fire safety rules and know what to do in an emergency.

blocks.heading: Use of rooms and equipment
blocks.body:
• Keep common areas and outdoor spaces tidy. Bicycles, strollers, and other equipment must not block escape routes.
• Keep your room clean and tidy.
• You must sort your waste and throw it into the correct bins.
• Common indoor areas must be cleaned regularly according to the cleaning schedules. You must clean when it is your turn.
• Washing machines, kitchen appliances, and cleaning equipment must be used according to their user manuals.

blocks.heading: Overnight visits
blocks.body:
• Overnight visits must always be arranged and approved by the reception centre in advance. The centre may deny the request.
• Adults may have overnight visits from their own children, as well as friends and family members living in other reception centres.
• Children under 18 may have overnight visits from parents, siblings, and peer friends.
• All overnight guests must reside in Norway and present a valid ID or asylum seeker certificate to the staff.
• You are responsible for providing food and bedding for your guests, and you must ensure they show consideration for other residents.

guide.title: Practical guide
guide.lead: The essentials of everyday life in Norway, explained briefly.

matavfall.name: Food waste
matavfall.bin: Green food bin. Always use a biodegradable bag and tie it. The bags are free in the shop and at the recycling station.
accepted: Kitchen paper with food on it
matavfall.note: No bio-bag? Wrap wet food waste in newspaper or a paper bag.

papir.name: Cardboard and paper
papir.bin: Blue paper bin. Put it in loose – NOT in a bag. Fold or cut up big boxes.
papir.note: It must be clean and dry. Rinse and flatten milk cartons.

plast.name: Plastic packaging
plast.bin: A separate transparent bag – tie it shut. The bag is free in the shop and at the recycling station.
rejected: Plastic with lots of food on it that will not wash off
plast.note: Rinse the packaging and let it dry. If it stays dirty, put it in residual waste.

glassmetall.name: Glass and metal packaging
glassmetall.bin: Shared bin for glass and metal. Put it in loose – NOT in a bag. Rinse it first.
accepted: Glass bottles from medicine, beer and wine
Jam jars and other food jars
Beer and soda cans without Norwegian deposit
rejected: Window glass, light bulbs, porcelain and mirrors
glassmetall.note: Only packaging (what the food or drink came in). Pots and pans go to the recycling station.

restavfall.name: Residual waste
restavfall.bin: The residual waste bin at home. Pack it in a bag and tie it well.
accepted: Bag of dog waste and cat litter
rejected: Food, paper, plastic, glass and metal – these must be sorted
restavfall.note: Residual waste is what is LEFT once you have sorted out the rest. Sort as much as you can first – it costs less.

farlig.name: Hazardous waste and electronics
farlig.bin: NEVER in any bin at home. Take batteries, light bulbs and small electronics to the shop that sells them, or to the recycling station.
accepted: Phones, chargers, cables and small electronics
Paint, glue, varnish and spray cans
rejected: Never in the toilet, sink or drain
farlig.note: Batteries in residual waste can catch fire in the rubbish truck.
